title: Dog Dragging Back Paws While Walking: Causes and Triage Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog dragging their back feet? Learn about knuckling, why it happens, and the urgency of veterinary care for hind limb mobility issues.
content_html:
TL;DR: Paw dragging, or knuckling, is a serious neurological sign that requires a veterinary evaluation within 24 hours, or immediate emergency care if your dog cannot walk or is in severe pain.

What does it mean if my dog is dragging their back paws or "knuckling"?

Dragging the back paws, often referred to by veterinarians as "knuckling," occurs when a dog walks on the tops of their feet instead of the pads. This behavior typically indicates a deficit in proprioception—the body's ability to sense its position in space. It is often a sign of an underlying neurological or orthopedic condition affecting the spinal cord or nerves in the hind end.

Is paw dragging in dogs considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Medium Urgency: Most cases of knuckling require a veterinary evaluation within 24 hours.
  • High Emergency: Seek immediate care if the dragging is accompanied by a sudden inability to walk, signs of extreme pain, or a loss of bladder control.
  • These symptoms are often signs of severe spinal cord compression and require urgent intervention.

How does a photo or video of my dog walking help the triage team?

  • A video shows the behavior in a natural environment, preventing adrenaline from masking symptoms as often happens at the clinic.
  • It allows the veterinarian to accurately assess your dog's gait and determine which specific nerves are likely affected.
  • The visual evidence helps the team prioritize your pet's care based on the actual severity of the motor loss shown.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Peripheral nerve injuries, such as those affecting the sciatic nerve or its branches, are common neuropathies in animals and can result in a dog dragging its back paws. These injuries may arise from pelvic fractures, intramedullary pin placement in the femur, or injections of irritating substances near the nerve. Damage to the proximal sciatic nerve can cause the dog to bear little or no weight on the affected limb, resulting in the dorsal surface of the paw dragging on the ground. Lumbosacral disease, resulting from degeneration and protrusion of the L7-S1 disc, hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum, or rarely subluxation of the lumbosacral joint, can also cause pelvic limb lameness and difficulty using the pelvic limbs, with potential proprioceptive deficits or muscle atrophy. In cases of peripheral nerve injury, amputation may be necessary due to damage from dragging or self-mutilation. For lumbosacral disease, dogs exhibiting mild pain may improve with rest.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1244)

faqs.question: What are the common causes of hind paw dragging?
faqs.answer: Common causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), Degenerative Myelopathy (DM), spinal cord injuries, or severe arthritis.

faqs.question: Can paw dragging be cured?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. Some conditions respond well to rest and medication, while others may require surgery or physical therapy.

faqs.question: What should I do while waiting for my vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Restrict your dog's movement immediately. Use a crate or a small room to prevent further injury and avoid using stairs or allowing them to jump on furniture.
